coredata.dc:title: Two kinds of biomass-derived carbon dots with one-step synthesis for Fe3+ and tetracyclines detection
coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Haiyan
Liu, Chuntong
Jing, Jing
Jing, Tao
Zhang, Xiaohong
Li, Jinlong
Luo, Chao
Qiu, Lixin
Li, Qiuying
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we developed an eco-friendly approach by utilizing industrial hemps as the carbon source to synthesize two kinds of CDs by one-pot hydrothermal method. We observed that the excitation wavelength showed dependence on CDs concentration in small CDs (S-CDs), not in big CDs (B-CDs). Further study showed that the B-CDs can be utilized for detection Fe3+ with the detection limit as low as 0.47 μmol L−1. Instead, our S-CDs could be specifically identified tetracycline antibiotics (TC, CTC, OTC) with the detection limits as 0.0330 μmol L−1, 0.27 μmol L−1 and 0.28 μmol L−1, respectively. In addition, we have successfully applied their application in actual sample (bottled drinking water and facial mask) to detect Fe3+ and TC, indicating their practicality. Overall, we have demonstrated that B-CDs and S-CDs detected Fe3+ and tetracycline antibiotics as powerful sensors and their potential applications in food and cosmetics detection of Fe3+ and TC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Two kinds of carbon dots
One-pot hydrothermal method
Industrial hemps
Fe3+ detection
Tetracycline detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of our B-CDs and S-CDs
Detection of tetracyclines antibiotics by S-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A, C) HRTEM images of B-CDs and S-CDs. (B, D) size distribution of B-CDs and S-CDs. (E, F) XRD and FTIR of B-CDs and S-CDs.

(A) XPS survey scan of B-CDs and S-CDs. (B) Element distribution of B-CDs and S-CDs. (C, D) C1s of B-CDs and S-CDs. (E, F) O1s of B-CDs and S-CDs. (G, H) N1s of B-CDs and S-CDs. (I, J) Si2p of B-CDs and S-CDs.
Schematic diagram of the possible structures of the B-CDs and S-CDs.

(A) The relationship between the concentration (100 μg/ml-12000 μg/ml) of B-CDs and S-CDs with the optimal excitation wavelength. (B) CIE of S-CDs under various concentrations. (C) 3D fluorescence matrices of S-CDs.

(A, B) Absorbance, excitation, and emission spectra of B-CDs and S-CDs. (Insets: Color of B-CDs under natural and UV light). (C) CIE of B-CDs. (D) CIE of S-CDs (2000 μg/mL). (E, F) Normalized fluorescence spectra of B-CDs and S-CDs at different excited wavelength.
(A–C) Salt tolerance, comparison of the photostability and the pH sensitivity of B-CDs and S-CDs, respectively.
Response of B-CDs to Fe3+ (A) FL intensity with concentration of Fe3+ (B) Fitting curve between (F0–F)/F0 and Fe3+ concentration.
Photographs of the B-CDs solution with the addition of Fe3+ (1–70 μmol L−1) in Cuvette under ultraviolet.
Specificity of B-CDs to Fe3+ detection (A) metal ions, (B) Amino acids and small organic molecules, (C) Antibiotics.

Response of S-CDs to TCs (A) FL intensity with concentration of TC. (B) Fitting curve between (F0–F)/F0 and TC concentration. (C) FL intensity with concentration of CTC. (D) Fitting curve between (F0–F)/F0 and CTC concentration. (E) FL intensity with various concentration of OTC. (F) Fitting curve between (F0–F)/F0 and OTC concentration.
Photographs of S-CDs fluorescence at different TC concentrations under 365 nm UV light.
Specificity of B-CDs to TCs detection (A) metal ions, (B) Amino acids and small organic molecules, (C) Antibiotics.
Sensing mechanism of CDs. (A) Fluorescence decay curves of B-CDs and B-CDs + Fe3+. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of B-CDs and B-CDs + Fe3+.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of TC/CTC/OTC and excitation and emission spectra of S-CDs. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of S-CDs and S-CDs + TC/CTC/OTC. (C) Fluorescence decay curves of S-CDs and S-CDs + TCs.
Determination of Fe3+ that added in bottled drinking water samples.
Determination of TC, CTC, OTC that added in facial mask samples.

head.ce:title: Two kinds of biomass-derived carbon dots with one-step synthesis for Fe3+ and tetracyclines detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we developed an eco-friendly approach by utilizing industrial hemps as the carbon source to synthesize two kinds of CDs by one-pot hydrothermal method. We observed that the excitation wavelength showed dependence on CDs concentration in small CDs (S-CDs), not in big CDs (B-CDs). Further study showed that the B-CDs can be utilized for detection Fe3+ with the detection limit as low as 0.47 μmol L−1. Instead, our S-CDs could be specifically identified tetracycline antibiotics (TC, CTC, OTC) with the detection limits as 0.0330 μmol L−1, 0.27 μmol L−1 and 0.28 μmol L−1, respectively. In addition, we have successfully applied their application in actual sample (bottled drinking water and facial mask) to detect Fe3+ and TC, indicating their practicality. Overall, we have demonstrated that B-CDs and S-CDs detected Fe3+ and tetracycline antibiotics as powerful sensors and their potential applications in food and cosmetics detection of Fe3+ and TC.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron is one of the essential elements to human body. Iron deficiency could cause a range of dysfunctions of the central nervous system, including motional, physical and cognitive, and other disorders. On the other hand, excess iron also caused various illnesses, such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's [1]. Therefore, the detection of iron has great significance in early diagnosis of these diseases.

Tetracycline antibiotics include tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and etc. [2]. Tetracycline antibiotics are low-cost and widely abused in recent years. However, they were easily absorbed by the human body and long-term exposure to tetracycline could contribute to some adverse reactions and diseases, including liver and kidney damage, allergy and toxic reactions, and yellow teeth [3]. Nowadays, people paid more attention to cosmetics safety due to the contamination accidents caused by illegally antibiotics addition. For example, anti-acne cosmetic products containing antibiotics have caused drug resistance and hypersensitivity. Therefore, it is imperative to examine the trace of tetracycline antibiotics in cosmetic products.

Previously, multiple traditional methods were developed for detecting iron, including atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [4], X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy (XFS) [5], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [6] and so on. To detect tetracycline (TC), various techniques, including high performance liquid chromatography [7], capillary electrochromatography-mass spectrometry [8], enzyme immunoassay [9], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [10], were employed. However, these analytical methods require delicate instruments and professional operators, creating huge obstacles for practical utilization. Instead, fluorescence sensor analysis could avoid the above shortcomings by its advantages including simple operation, rapid analysis, strong stability, and high selectivity and specificity. Among all fluorescent materials, CDs have low toxicity and can detect Fe3+ ions and TCs and record their change in complex environment.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a novel fluorescent nanomaterial which have the advantages, including easy synthesis and modification, good biocompatibility, and less cytotoxicity [11]. Therefore, they have been commonly used in many fields, including cell imaging [12], photocatalysis [13], fingerprint recognition [14], drug carriers [15], optical sensing [16], and other fields. Currently, various procedures were developed to synthesize CDs, for examples, microwave method [17], thermo decomposition [18], electrochemistry [19], hydrothermal method [20] and so on. Among all the methods, the hydrothermal method is the most used, not only because of its more friendly and convenient experimental environment, but also the particle sizes of its prepared CDs are uniform [21].

Now different types of biowastes such as orange juice [22], Pomelo peel [23], natural red beet pigment [24], tobacco [25], rice residue [26] were used as the carbon source for CDs preparation. The biowastes were not only abundant and nontoxic, but also easily obtained and environmentally friendly. Industrial hemps containing rich protein, fiber, and carbohydrates have been used as carbon sources for CDs. However, the application from industrial hemp synthesized CDs to detect iron and TCs has not been reported yet.

In this essay, we used industrial hemps as carbon source to synthesize CDs, and simultaneously obtained both big CDs (B-CDs) and small CDs (S-CDs) by one-pot hydrothermal method. Compared with B-CDs, the optimal excitation wavelength of S-CDs was dependent on their concentration. Further study showed that the B-CDs could be utilized for Fe3+ detection and the S-CDs can specifically identify tetracycline antibiotics (Tetracycline (TC), Chlortetracycline (CTC) and Oxytetracycline (OTC)). Moreover, both CDs have been evaluated in actual samples (bottled drinking water and facial mask) to detect Fe3+ ions and TCs, indicating their high sensitivity and selectivity.

HRTEM images indicated that both B-CDs and S-CDs were spherical and uniformly dispersed (Fig. 1 A and C). The average particle size of B-CDs and S-CDs was 6.838 ± 0.8910 nm (Figs. 1B), and 1.978 ± 0.2783 nm (Fig. 1D), respectively. In addition, XRD of B-CDs and S-CDs showed only one obvious diffraction peak near 23° (Fig. 1E), which was corresponded to their amorphous structure. Considering the difference of particle size between two CDs, the quantum size effect could be one of the fundamental reasons for the separation of both CDs.

To better understand the contrast between B-CDs and S-CDs, we used FTIR to characterize their structures (Fig. 1F). Both CDs shared some peaks among 1250 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. For example, there was a strong and broad peak near 3440 cm−1 which was corresponded to N–H, O–H the stretching vibration on the surface of the CDs [27]. Two characteristic peaks near 2970 cm−1 and 2940 cm−1 were associated with C–H stretching vibration. Moreover, the medium-strength peaks at 1600 cm−1 was related to CC/CO/CN stretching vibration [27,28]. A weak absorption peak appears near 1410 cm−1, which might be caused by -C-N = /C–N [28,29]. However, the intensity of the peak at 1100 cm−1 which was related to Si–O–Si stretching vibration [30,31] which changed significantly. The intensity of the peak in B-CDs was much stronger than it in S-CDs because of more Si–O–Si contents in B-CDs. Furthermore, compared with S-CDs, an additional absorption peak of B-CDs was observed at 802 cm−1, suggesting B-CDs probably contained Si–C stretching vibration and Si–O bending vibration [32,33]. Overall, these changes in the peaks of B-CDs and S-CDs in the low wavenumber range indicated that there were indeed differences between their structures, suggesting it was one of the fundamental reasons for their different properties.

To explore more detailed information of the surface functional groups in both CDs, we employed XPS to analyze their surface structure (Fig. 2 ). XPS analysis indicated that both B-CDs and S-CDs contained five elements: C, N, O, Si, and Ca, which were corresponded to the peak at 284.8 eV, 399.2 eV, 531.6 eV, 102.3 eV and 346.85 eV, respectively (Fig. 2A). We noted that Ca in both CDs existed in calcium carbonate form. In addition, the percentage of each element in both CDs was calculated (Fig. 2B). Next, high-resolution XPS spectra analysis for each element were performed. For both CDs, the C1s spectra displayed three peaks at 284.0 eV, 284.8 eV and 287.5 eV, which were corresponded to C–C/C–Si/CC, C–N/C–O and CN [34,35], respectively (Fig. 2C and D). The O1s spectra were decomposed into three peaks at 530.4 eV, 531.7 eV and 532.7 eV, which were ascribed to CO, O–H, and C–O/Si–O [34,36], respectively (Fig. 2E and F). Three fitted characteristic peaks at 398.5 eV, 399.2 eV and 400.9 eV were observed in the N1s spectra, indicating the existence of C–N, CN and N–H [32,37], respectively (Fig. 2G and H). Si 2p decomposition peaks of B-CDs displayed three peaks at 101.2 eV, 102.4 eV and 103.7 eV, which were associated with Si–O–Si, C–Si and Si–O [29,32,33], respectively (Fig. 2I). Instead, only one peak at 101.6 eV, which was ascribed to Si–O–Si [32] in S-CDs (Fig. 2J). Altogether, these results were consistent with the findings obtained by infrared spectroscopy (Fig. 1F).

In all, we conjectured that the surface of both CDs contained CO, –OH, –NH2 and silicon-containing groups, indicating their easy modification and high water solubility. Their possible structures were shown in Fig. 3 .

Interestingly, we found that the optimal excitation wavelengths of S-CDs were dependent on their concentration at high concentrations (Fig. 4 A), B-CDs was not. When the concentration of S-CDs was increased, the excitation wavelength and the corresponding emission wavelength shown redshift (Fig. 4B). In accordance, CIE color coordinates of S-CDs gradually moved from blue to green.

To further investigate the color tunable change, we plotted 3D fluorescence matrices of S-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4C, the excitation wavelength and fluorescence intensity changed with the concentration of S-CDs altered. Specifically, when the S-CDs concentration was 2000 μg/mL, the fluorescence intensity reached to the highest with the corresponding the excitation wavelength at 350 nm. The result shown that the fluorescence intensity of S-CDs elevated and then diminished with the increased concentration, suggesting the self-quenching effect caused by the continuous increase of the concentration which lead to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity [38].

Previous studies revealed that the concentration-dependent fluorescence of CDs could be caused by the interaction among particles [39]. In our experiment, the surface of S-CDs contained CO, –OH, NH2 and Si-containing groups. At low concentration, the interparticle distance increased and resulted in blue-shift [40], leading escalating fluorescence intensity. However, while at high concentration, the interparticle distance kept decreasing and causing the aggregation of surface functional groups in S-CDs, reducing the energy gap, and leading to the red-shift of λ ex and λ em [41], showing declined fluorescence intensity. In conclusion, we selected the S-CDs concentration in 2000 μg/mL for our subsequent experiments to exceed its best specificity and sensitivity.

As for B-CDs, there were two weak peaks at 270 nm and 320 nm (Fig. 5 A), indicating possible transitions containing π→π* and n→π* electron energy levels in B-CDs. This finding also corresponded to the results obtained by FTIR and XPS. We observed λ ex and λ em of B-CDs at 445 nm and 500 nm (Fig. 5A). Fig. 5A inset shows that the CDs solution exhibited colorless under sunlight, then exhibited an obvious cyan-blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV light which was consistent with the color range shown in CIE color coordinate (0.2367, 0.4203) (Fig. 5C).

As for S-CDs, two absorbing peaks at 270 nm and 320 nm were detected (Fig. 5B). As for S-CDs, two absorbing peaks at 270 nm and 320 nm were detected (Fig. 5B), suggesting the existence of π→π* and n→π*, correlated to CC and CO bonds. The λ ex and λ em were spotted at 350 nm and 435 nm, respectively (Fig. 5B). S-CDs showed a bright blue fluorescence under UV light (insets in Fig. 5B) which was also accorded with CIE color coordinate (0.1572, 0.1247) (Fig. 5D).

Both B-CDs and S-CDs had excitation wavelength dependence (Fig. 5E and F). Since B-CDs and S-CDs were prepared from natural product industrial hemp which contained several compounds, they could both have complex structures which led to the molecular transition mode change under different excitation wavelengths and eventually caused the emission wavelength shift.

In addition, we calculated the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of B-CDs and S-CDs as 6.62% and 25.05%, respectively. The significant difference of QY between B-CDs and S-CDs was probably caused by their particle diameter dissimilarity.

To investigate the fluorescence stability, we decided to explore the fluorescent emission intensity under different conditions, for instance optical stability, ion strength, and pH. When the ion concentration was at 1.0 mol L−1, the fluorescence intensity of the two CDs didn't show significant change, suggesting their excellent tolerance in ion solutions (Fig. 6 A). Under ultraviolet light (365 nm) irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs kept stable for at least 60 min. Instead, the fluorescence intensity of S-CDs had a slightly decrease starting at 10 min, and kept stable after 20 min (Fig. 6B). It is of significance to examine the fluorescence stability of B-CDs and S-CDs under different pH conditions because pH is a key factor in actual detection. The intensity of B-CDs increased when pH value increased from 1 to 5, and remained unchanged from 5 to 8, then dramatically decreased when pH value raised from 9 to 13 (Fig. 6C). Similarly, the fluorescence stability of S-CDs was slightly changed with pH value ranging from 1 to 8, and then also decreased when pH value raised from 9 to 13. Altogether, we found that two CDs have obvious differences in optical properties. Previous researches proved that quantum size effect, surface states and molecular states could affect the optical properties of CDs. In our study, our B-CDs and S-CDs had similar surface structures, so we speculated the differences in optical performances of B-CDs and S-CDs might be attributed to quantum size effects [42]. In briefly, the particle size of S-CDs was smaller so that they had larger specific area, more denser surface groups, more surface energy, and thus high activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of our B-CDs and S-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra were recorded while various concentrations Fe3+ ions were added into the B-CDs solution. As shown in Fig. 7 A, as the concentration of Fe3+ increased from 0 μmol L−1 to 100 μmol L−1, the fluorescence intensity shown a gradual decline. In the range of 0–10 μmol L−1, 10–30 μmol L−1 and 30–60 μmol L−1, they showed perfect linear relationship with fitted linear equations as (F0–F)/F0 = 0.02203 [Fe3+] + 0.00403 (R2 = 0.99541), (F0–F)/F0 = 0.00716 [Fe3+] + 0.15722 (R2 = 0.99225) and (F0–F)/F0 = 0.01312 [Fe3+]-0.01204 (R2 = 0.99363), respectively (Fig. 7B). The detection limit of B-CDs to Fe3+ (S/N = 3) was as low as 0.47 μmol L−1. We also compared our results with previous studies and summarized them in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tetracyclines antibiotics by S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we decided to inspect the performance of our S-CDs with various concentrations of tetracyclines (Tetracycline (TC), Chlortetracycline (CTC), Oxytetracycline (OTC)) detection. While the tetracyclines concentrations was increased from 0 μmol L−1 to 50 μmol L−1, the fluorescence intensity of S-CDs gradually decreased (Fig. 10 A, C, 10E). Specifically, when the TC concentration was in the range of 0–10 μmol L−1, 10–35 μmol L−1 and 35–50 μmol L−1, they showed a good linear relationship with fitted linear equations as (F0–F)/F0 = 0.0455 [TC]-0.01103 (R2 = 0.99019), (F0–F)/F0 = 0.01669 [TC] + 0.30994 (R2 = 0.99637) and (F0–F)/F0 = 0.00537 [TC] + 0.70961 (R2 = 0.98085), with the detection limit for TC (S/N = 3) at 0.0330 μmol L−1 (Fig. 10B). When the CTC concentration was in the range of 0–30 μmol L−1 and 30–55 μmol L−1, they also shown perfect linear relationship with fitted linear equations as (F0–F)/F0 = 0.01923 [CTC]-0.00306 (R2 = 0.99932) and (F0–F)/F0 = 0.00987 [CTC]+0.37644 (R2 = 0.99233), with the detection limit of S-CDs for CTC as 0.27 μmol L−1 (Fig. 10D). Lastly, the relationship between (F0–F)/F0 and OTC concentration was also calculated (Fig. 10F). In the range of 0–10 μmol L−1 and 10–40 μmol L−1, they showed a perfect linear relationship with fitted linear equations as (F0–F)/F0 = 0.0422 [OTC]-0.00403 (R2 = 0.99799) and (F0–F)/F0 = 0.01595 [OTC]+0.27393 (R2 = 0.99639). The detection limit of S-CDs for OTC was 0.28 μmol L−1. Moreover, we compared our CDs to previous studies and concluded that our S-CDs sensor system presented a wide linear range with low LOD in detecting tetracyclines (Table S2).

To explore quenching mechanism about B-CDs and Fe3+ ion, we performed the fluorescence lifetime and UV absorption experiments. The average fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs was 4.72 ns. With the presence of Fe3+, we didn't detect obvious change of the average fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 13 A). However, the absorption peak intensity of B-CDs at 320 nm was disappeared with Fe3+ addition, indicating a possible reaction between Fe3+ and –OH group on the surface of B-CDs (Fig. 13B). Therefore, we suspected that fluorescence quenching mechanism of B-CDs/Fe3+ system could be the static quenching.

With the presence of tetracycline antibiotics, UV spectra of TC showed that a broad band overlapped with the excitation band of S-CDs around 350 nm (Fig. 14 A). Furthermore, the absorption peaks intensity was escalated with the presence of TC with no change of the location of peaks, implying no new substances were produced between TCs and S-CDs (Fig. 14B). As Fig. 14C shown no evident change in the decay curve after TCs addition. The average lifetime of S-CDs was calculated as 3.79 ns. After the addition of TCs (TC, CTC and OTC) to the S-CDs solution, the average lifetime was approximately 3.75 ns, 3.13 ns and 3.41 ns, respectively. Together, our results suggested that fluorescence quenching of S-CDs was possibly caused by the internal filtering effect (IFE) with the existence of TCs. The mechanism diagram of IFE was shown in Fig. 15 .

In this experiment, we used industrial hemps as the carbon source and obtained two CDs (B-CDs and S-CDs) with similar structures but different particle sizes using a one-pot hydrothermal method at 170 °C for 10 h. B-CDs showed excitation wavelength dependence, good salt tolerance, light stability, strong response to Fe3+. Besides, the S-CDs exhibited excitation wavelength dependence and induced polychromatic luminescence under concentration regulation. Meanwhile S-CDs displayed more acid and alkali resistance, and sensitivity and efficiency for the detection of tetracycline antibiotics. Furthermore, our B-CDs and S-CDs had good stability and low detection limits. Besides, our prepared CDs (B-CDs and S-CDs) have also been successfully applied in the detection of metal ions and tetracycline antibiotics in actual samples. In summary, we believe our CDs (B-CDs and S-CDs) would have good application prospects in environmental water analysis and other actual sample detection in the future.

Haiyan Qi: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Methodology, All authors made substantial contributions to this paper. Chuntong Liu: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, All authors made substantial contributions to this paper. Jing Jing: Investigation, Data curation, All authors made substantial contributions to this paper. Tao Jing: Validation, All authors made substantial contributions to this paper. Xiaohong Zhang: Funding acquisition, All authors made substantial contributions to this paper. Jinlong Li: Supervision, All authors made substantial contributions to this paper. Chao Luo: Software, Resources. Lixin Qiu: Software, Resources, All authors made substantial contributions to this paper. Qiuying Li: Proofreading manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172092); the Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (145109507,135109207); the Program for Science and technology of Qiqihar (GYGG-201903).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110555.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal chelator EGCG attenuates Fe(III)-induced conformational transition of α-synuclein and protects AS-PC12 cells against Fe(III)-induced death

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao J, Xu LH, Liang QN, Sun Q, Chen CH, Zhang Y, et al. Metal chelator EGCG attenuates Fe(III)-induced conformational transition of α-synuclein and protects AS-PC12 cells against Fe(III)-induced death. Neurochem 2017;143(1):136-146.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dissipation kinetics of oxytetracycline, tetracycline, and chlortetracycline residues in soil

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li YZ, Wang HJ, Liu XX, Zhao GY, Sun Y. Dissipation kinetics of oxytetracycline, tetracycline, and chlortetracycline residues in soil. Environ Sci Pollut R 2016;23:13822-13831.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Addressing resistance to antibiotics in systematic reviews of antibiotic interventions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Leibovici L, Paul M, Garner P, Sinclair DJ, Afshari AP, Pace NL, et al. Addressing resistance to antibiotics in systematic reviews of antibiotic interventions. J Antimicrob Chemoth 2016;71(9):2367-2369.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Method development for the determination of iron in milligram amounts of rice plants (Oryza sativa L.) from cultivation experiments using graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Silva M, Vale M, Damin I, Welz B, Mandaji M, Fett J. Method development for the determination of iron in milligram amounts of rice plants (Oryza sativa L.) from cultivation experiments using graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry. Anal Bioanal Chem 2003;377:165-172.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Assessment of the elemental profile of leafy vegetables by synchrotron-radiation-induced energy dispersive X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bharti AS, Sharma S, Singh AK, Tiwari MK, Uttam KN. Assessment of the Elemental Profile of Leafy Vegetables by Synchrotron-Radiation-Induced Energy Dispersive X-Ray Fluorescence Spectroscopy. J Appl Spectrosc+ 2021;88:653-661.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Measurement of haem and total iron in fish, shrimp and prawn using ICP-MS: implications for dietary iron intake calculations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wheal MS, DeCourcy-Ireland E, Bogard JR, Thilsted SH, Stangoulis JCR. Measurement of haem and total iron in fish, shrimp and prawn using ICP-MS: Implications for dietary iron intake calculations. Food Chem 2016;201(15):222-229.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: HPLC separation of tetracycline analogues: comparison study of laser-based polarimetric detection with UV detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ng K, Linder SW. HPLC Separation of Tetracycline Analogues: Comparison Study of Laser-Based Polarimetric Detection with UV Detection. J Chromatogr Sci 2003; 41(9):460-466.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analysis of tetracycline residues in bovine milk by CE-MS with field-amplified sample stacking
